## [00:00 - 03:04]

Hi, this is Don Crowther with a video about instructional content. So now we're moving into stage three in our webinar, and it's taking about 15 to 20 minutes in a 60-minute webinar, or 25 to 35 minutes in a 90-minute webinar. Now note something important here. In a 60-minute webinar, we're only spending about a fourth to a third of it actually teaching content. And I'll tell you, that's key. Because remember, the purpose of this webinar is not to teach some stuff. It's to sell them stuff. And one mistake that most people make when they try to sell them stuff using a webinar is that they teach far too much. As opposed to spending more time setting up with the reframer, the opportunity setting up with your story, setting up with the introduction done properly, and then moving into a sales portion. And the sales portion is going to take time to sell correctly. It takes time. Every time. So I strongly encourage you to avoid the natural inclination of, I got to teach more. Well, teaching more rarely sells more. In fact, it almost always hurts your sales. So the purpose of this is to give them enough information to start down the path towards goal without being able to go all the way to its end. That's one of the challenges that comes in this process is that we want to give them lots of stuff, which tends to push them towards being able to go all the way to the end without having to buy our product. And we need to provide the three key proofs that they need to have before they're comfortable buying your product. So once again, even our content portion is working on getting them to the mindset that they need to have to be able to buy your product. So what are those three key proofs to successfully sell information? There are three things that you the seller must prove to the buyer. And almost always when I look at a webinar that's not working, I find that we're not doing at least one of these sufficiently. Number one, can I this is from the standpoint of the audience, the attendee, can I learn and implement this stuff? This is particularly important if the process is complex, if it's technical, if there are lots of steps, if it's hard work, they need to understand whether or not they can actually

## [03:04 - 06:05]

learn it and then be able to implement it. I will tell you, this is a big piece for a lot of things, especially if you're dealing with an audience that isn't as likely to be willing to and able to take on the large amounts of mental and or physical work necessary to accomplish something. Second key proof, are you the right person to teach me this thing as opposed to someone else? Almost everyone who is selling something has competitors. And so their audience has people that they could go buy a very similar product to theirs from. And so you need to establish in this process that you are the you are their person. That they should be buying from you because you're the best person to teach them specifically. Now realize as soon as you do that, that there are people who you're not their person. They should go to someone else. And that's just fine. By the way, that's why we also do joint venture promotions to other people's products is that sometimes the people who aren't your people should go someplace else to learn from them. And you might as well take a commission as they do that. Third key proof is and this is probably the one that most people don't do well enough at is the value that I receive worth the worth the price I'm required to pay. In other words, will my return that I'll get from taking this particular program justify the expense of the time, the money, the effort required to do it. So this content section deals with all three of those. We need to do helping them to get the understanding that yes, they can learn and implement. Yes, you are the right person to teach them. And yes, the value that they're going to receive is worth the price that they're going to pay. All right. All right. So in another video, I'm going to show you these five ways of teaching those that content and we'll cover each one of them in depth. This is how you're going to go about doing it. But I caution you to make sure as you do this that you provide them with great information. But don't leave them feeling overwhelmed. Don't leave them walking out saying it's going to take me weeks just to get that part done. I'm not going to buy until I'm done. Anytime someone says I'll wait, it means I'm not going to buy. You can never create that emotion in people. You also cannot leave them feeling satisfied. I know he's selling something really cool, but what he's taught is going to help me achieve

## [06:05 - 07:31]

so much out of the transformation that I don't need to buy his course right now. Remember, if they wait, they're not buying. So never get them enough that they feel like they could they ought to just go do this and then they'll see where they are. You must show in a webinar for it to be successful that the content that you're showing them is only a fraction of what they need to succeed and that they still need to do this and that and the other thing that needs to be obvious that they really do need to do those other things. Because again, many people watch a webinar here, an option to buy something for 500 or thousand or $2,000 or more. And they say, I'm just going to try this free stuff. Maybe that'll get me enough. Another killer thing is I'll just do the free stuff and hopefully that'll enable me to earn enough to be able to pay for the other program that you're offering. That's also a kiss of death. So. Needs to be obvious that in order to succeed, they have to buy your course.
